L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Francesco had only 1 BASE season despite 425 BASE jumps, indicating a compressed progression timeline. Slider-up cliff jumping in slick configuration (no tracking suit) from a terminal cliff requires well-developed sub-terminal or terminal tracking skills and refined exit technique built over time. The domain reference emphasizes that time in the sport is as important as jump numbers and that rushed progression is a recognized risk factor. One season of experience is insufficient for the judgment and site-specific skills needed for slider-up cliff objects, and this inexperience directly relates to the unstable exit that produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes Jeremiah as fixated on flying through the left notch, raising the topic repeatedly during the hike despite being told he lacked the data to assess it and being offered safer alternatives. This fixation on a single desired line narrowed his consideration of other options and contributed to his decision to commit to the unverified technical proximity line.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience for Task
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper attempted a technical proximity line requiring subterminal turning, precise terrain clearance assessment, and site-specific knowledge on his first visit to the object. With 150 WS BASE jumps and a 10-month gap since his last WS BASE jump, he lacked the site-specific experience and flight data that accepted practice requires before committing to such a technical line. The companion who had flown the notch once before emphasized it required laser data, GPS data, and prior flight experience to assess.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Self-Entrapment in Plan
L2_preconditions.description: The updated narrative describes the jumper displaying nervous energy at the exit (a 'nervous chakra' instead of a fist bump) and the companion's reflection that 'sometimes we trap ourselves into a plan.' The jumper appeared to have mentally committed to the notch line despite his uncertainty, and his nervousness at the exit point suggests he recognized the risk but felt compelled to proceed with the plan he had fixated on.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's last wingsuit BASE jump was approximately 10 months prior (November 2019). While he had made 200 wingsuit skydives in the interim, wingsuit skydiving does not replicate the specific demands of wingsuit BASE—particularly subterminal exits, terrain interaction, and start arc management. This extended gap in WS BASE currency degraded his ability to accurately assess his performance capabilities for a technical proximity line.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there is nothing to suggest any previous BASE experience or training, and the jumper held only an expired USPA A license. Without a first jump course or mentorship, the jumper lacked the fundamental knowledge that skydiving equipment is inappropriate for BASE jumping and that BASE-specific gear is required for sub-terminal deployments from fixed objects.

L2_preconditions.label: Adverse physiological condition
L2_preconditions.description: The report lists 'Health issues' as a possible factor. While details are not specified, this suggests a pre-existing physical or physiological condition that may have degraded the jumper's ability to execute the PC extraction, contributing to the inability to locate and pull the pilot chute.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 150 skydives, which is below the generally accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE. With 143 BASE jumps on insufficient foundational skydiving training, core skills including reliable BOC extraction under stress may not have been adequately developed, contributing to the failed pull attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was undertaken to honor Norway's Independence Day, creating a ceremonial motivation to complete a jump that day. After the original 930-foot exit was aborted due to security, and the second jumper elected not to jump from the lower level, the jumper proceeded anyway — suggesting the commemorative purpose and commitment to the endeavor drove the decision to accept a clearly inadequate exit point rather than abort entirely.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was making his first BASE jump from a terminal slider-up cliff (Trollveggen). Accepted progression norms require building exit and deployment skills at simpler objects (spans, shorter cliffs) before attempting terminal cliff objects. A first BASE jump from a terminal cliff represents a clear insufficiency in BASE experience that directly contributed to the unstable exit and deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The delayed brake release after a riser correction is a fundamental canopy handling sequence that requires practiced muscle memory. With only 10 jumps, the jumper had minimal opportunity to develop the reflexive riser-to-brake transition needed for prompt heading corrections, directly contributing to the slow brake release that cost critical altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 10 BASE jumps, the jumper lacked the experience to accurately assess canopy performance relative to remaining altitude and distance to the landing area. This inexperience contributed to the misjudgment that he could clear the dangerous pool and reach the main landing zone when he was already too low, rather than selecting a closer emergency landing option.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, no experienced jumper with him, and no apparent skydiving background. He completely lacked the prerequisite skydiving experience, first jump course, and mentorship that constitute accepted practice for BASE jumping.

L2_preconditions.label: No freefall or exit skills
L2_preconditions.description: With no BASE training, no BASE jumps, and no reported skydiving experience, the jumper lacked any freefall body position skills or exit technique. This complete absence of trained capability directly contributed to the unstable exit, inability to maintain orientation, and failure to achieve any separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was focused on filming two other jumpers while in a back-to-earth position, channelizing attention on the camera task to the exclusion of altitude awareness. The narrative explicitly identifies 'focusing on filming' as a factor and notes this is the second fatality involving flying back-to-earth with a camera, indicating the filming task diverted attention from monitoring deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was in a back-to-earth position, which eliminates visual reference to the ground and impairs the ability to perceive altitude and proximity to terrain. This body orientation directly degraded the jumper's spatial awareness, contributing to the over-delay and late pilot chute extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Unfamiliar container not adequately prepared for
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was flying a new LD5 container he had never jumped before, which had a different BOC placement than his previous Zack rig. The witness confirmed that the LD5 BOC was difficult to reach if body position wasn't 100% correct. Jumping a new container system on a wingsuit BASE jump without first verifying BOC accessibility (e.g., through practice pulls or skydiving the rig) constitutes inadequate equipment preparation that directly enabled the failed PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was 'not very current at the moment' — he had been focusing on a paragliding course that year rather than BASE jumping, despite being highly current in previous years. Reduced currency degraded the motor skill proficiency needed to execute a smooth BOC extraction from an unfamiliar container under wingsuit flight conditions, directly contributing to the failed deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's decision to stow the PC in the BOC pouch for a static line jump violates a fundamental and explicitly stated rule of static line rigging ('PC must never be stowed during static line jump'). This specific error reflects a knowledge gap about how forces act on a stowed PC during static line deployment — knowledge that accepted BASE training and proper mentorship would have provided.
